topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
researcher_bg: HCI/visualization researcher studying explainable AI for virtual cell models and gene prediction workflows
expert_bg: Molecular biologist conducting gene perturbation experiments and evaluating virtual cell model reliability

shared_root.label: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
shared_root.description: Interview topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

exp_L1_00.label: Model Reliability & Biological Fidelity
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether virtual cell models accurately reflect real biology and can be trusted for experimental design or interpretation.

exp_L2_00_00.label: Predictive Accuracy Gaps
exp_L2_00_00.description: Discrepancies between model predictions and wet-lab outcomes that undermine confidence.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Predictions fail on novel gene combinations
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The model nails single-gene knockouts but falls apart when I try double or triple perturbations—especially with genes from different pathways. It’s like it’s memorizing known interactions instead of understanding mechanism.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Cell-type specificity is faked
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: The model claims to be trained on iPSC-derived neurons, but the outputs look suspiciously generic—like it’s just regurgitating HEK293 patterns. I can’t tell if it actually captures tissue-specific regulation.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Temporal dynamics feel off
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: When I simulate a time course after CRISPRi, the model shows immediate effects—but in reality, protein turnover takes hours. It ignores biological lag, which screws up my dosing schedule.

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Can't distinguish direct vs. indirect effects
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: The model flags dozens of downstream genes as 'perturbed' but gives no clue whether they’re primary targets or just noise from cascade effects. That makes follow-up validation a nightmare.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Training Data Limitations
exp_L2_00_01.description: Issues stemming from the quality, scope, or bias in data used to train the virtual cell model.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Over-reliance on overexpressed systems
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Most training data comes from forced overexpression—not physiological levels—so the model doesn’t know what ‘normal’ looks like. It’s like learning human behavior from reality TV.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Missing rare cell states
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: If a cell state only appears in 0.1% of scRNA-seq datasets—like early stress responses—it’s invisible to the model. But those are often the most interesting biologically!

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Batch effects baked into predictions
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: I ran the same perturbation through two versions of CellForge trained on different consortia data, and got opposite results. Turns out the model learned platform-specific artifacts, not biology.

exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: No representation of genetic background diversity
exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: All training cells are from European ancestry lines. When I test in diverse iPSCs, the model’s confidence plummets—or worse, stays high while being wrong.

exp_L2_00_02.label: Intuitive Mistrust of Black-Box Behavior
exp_L2_00_02.description: Gut feelings that something’s off, even when metrics look okay.

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Outputs feel 'too clean'
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: Real biology is messy—noise, outliers, partial penetrance—but the model gives crisp, deterministic answers. That smoothness feels artificial, like it’s oversimplifying stochasticity.

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Confidence scores don’t match my experience
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: The model says it’s 95% sure about a prediction I’ve seen fail three times in the lab. Its calibration feels disconnected from experimental reality.

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Can’t sense when the model is extrapolating
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: There’s no warning when I’m asking about a gene family it barely saw during training. It just blithely generates an answer like it knows what it’s doing.

exp_L1_01.label: Experimental Workflow Integration
exp_L1_01.description: Challenges in fitting virtual cell tools into existing lab routines without creating bottlenecks or friction.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Tool Usability & Accessibility
exp_L2_01_00.description: Barriers to actually using the model day-to-day in a busy lab.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Requires bioinformatics support to run
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: I can’t just type in a gene and hit go—I need someone to wrangle Docker containers and YAML configs. By the time it’s ready, I’ve already designed the experiment manually.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Slow turnaround for batch queries
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: Testing 50 sgRNAs? The web UI times out. Local install crashes. Meanwhile, my postdoc is waiting to order oligos.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Output isn’t formatted for lab notebooks
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: The JSON dump has 200 fields, but I just need: gene, expected phenotype, confidence, and top 3 off-targets. Everything else is noise I have to filter out.

exp_leaf_01_00_03.label: No version control for model runs
exp_leaf_01_00_03.description: I ran an experiment last month based on v1.2, but now v1.3 gives different results—and there’s no way to reproduce or compare. My PI asks why conclusions changed, and I can’t answer.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Misalignment with Wet-Lab Timelines
exp_L2_01_01.description: Timing mismatches between computational predictions and physical experiment cycles.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Predictions arrive too late for grant deadlines
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: I needed preliminary in silico data for my R01 submission, but the model queue was backed up for two weeks. Had to skip it and lose points on innovation.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Can’t iterate fast enough during troubleshooting
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: My CRISPR screen failed—was it the guide or the delivery? I need rapid what-if simulations to debug, but each query takes hours. By then, I’ve moved on.

exp_L2_01_02.label: Cognitive Load & Interpretation Burden
exp_L2_01_02.description: Mental effort required to translate model output into actionable decisions.

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Too many plausible-but-wrong hypotheses
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: The model gives me five mechanistic stories for why Gene X affects proliferation. All sound reasonable, but only one is testable with my current reagents. I waste days chasing ghosts.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: Hard to explain model logic to collaborators
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: When I present findings based on CellForge, my immunology collaborator asks, ‘But how does it *know*?’ and I can’t give a satisfying answer without diving into attention maps—which they don’t care about.

exp_L1_02.label: Validation & Ground Truth Challenges
exp_L1_02.description: Difficulties in verifying whether model predictions are correct due to limitations in experimental validation.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Gold Standard Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: Lack of clear benchmarks to judge model performance.

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: No consensus on ‘correct’ phenotype readout
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: Is apoptosis measured by Annexin V, caspase activity, or morphology? Different assays give different answers, so how do I score the model?

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Context-dependent ground truth
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: A gene knockout kills cells in serum-free media but not with FBS. Which condition defines ‘truth’ for the model? There’s no universal answer.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Cost & Feasibility of Validation
exp_L2_02_01.description: Practical barriers to testing model predictions experimentally.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Validating predictions eats my entire budget
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: The model suggests 20 high-confidence hits, but validating even five with orthogonal assays costs more than my quarterly reagent allowance.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Some predictions are untestable with current tech
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: It predicts changes in nuclear speckle dynamics—but we don’t have live imaging for that in our system. So the prediction sits there, unverifiable but haunting my to-do list.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Feedback Loop Deficiency
exp_L2_02_02.description: Inability to feed experimental results back to improve the model.

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: No easy way to report failed predictions
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: When the model gets it wrong, there’s no ‘report error’ button. My negative data vanishes instead of helping retrain the system.

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: My CO2 incubator runs hot, my FBS lot varies—these quirks affect outcomes, but the model assumes idealized conditions. My validation failures aren’t ‘real’ failures, just context drift.

exp_L1_03.label: Ethical, Regulatory & Governance Risks
exp_L1_03.description: Concerns about data provenance, compliance, and unintended consequences of model use.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Data Provenance & Consent
exp_L2_03_00.description: Uncertainty about whether training data was ethically sourced.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Don’t know if patient-derived data had proper consent for AI training
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: The model was trained on public scRNA-seq from cancer patients—but were they told their data might train commercial AI? I’m uncomfortable building on shaky ethics.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Commercial models hide training sources
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: CellForge Enterprise won’t disclose which datasets were used. For all I know, it includes proprietary pharma data I’m not allowed to touch under my grant terms.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Intellectual Property Ambiguity
exp_L2_03_01.description: Unclear ownership of predictions and derived discoveries.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Who owns a drug target discovered via CellForge?
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: If the model suggests a novel synthetic lethal pair that becomes a patent, does the AI vendor get a cut? My tech transfer office is sweating over this.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Auditability & Reproducibility Standards
exp_L2_03_02.description: Lack of frameworks to audit or certify model behavior for regulated work.

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Can’t use in GLP studies because model isn’t validated per FDA guidelines
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: We’re developing a cell therapy, but regulators won’t accept any design choices influenced by an unvalidated AI. So we ignore the tool entirely—even when it’s right.

exp_L1_04.label: Team Dynamics & Skill Gaps
exp_L1_04.description: Human and organizational challenges in adopting and collaborating around virtual cell models.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Training & Onboarding Deficits
exp_L2_04_00.description: Lack of resources to teach wet-lab biologists how to use or interpret AI tools.

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: Postdocs treat model output as gospel
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: New team members see a high-confidence prediction and skip controls—they don’t understand it’s a simulation, not data. I’ve had to redo entire experiments because of this.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: No cross-training between computational and wet-lab staff
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: My bioinformatician doesn’t know what a lentiviral titer is, and I don’t know what a latent space is. We talk past each other when debugging model-experiment mismatches.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Credit & Incentive Misalignment
exp_L2_04_01.description: Disincentives for sharing or improving model-based workflows.

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: Using AI doesn’t count toward first-author papers
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: Journals still want ‘wet-lab validation’ as the hero. If I base my story on CellForge, reviewers say it’s ‘just computational’—even if it guided every key experiment.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: PIs hoard custom model tweaks
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: One lab fine-tuned CellForge for hematopoietic cells and won’t share the weights—they treat it like a secret sauce. The field fragments instead of converging.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Emerging Role Confusion
exp_L2_04_02.description: Uncertainty about who is responsible for what in AI-augmented science.

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: Who debugs when model + experiment disagree?
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: Is it the biologist’s fault for a bad assay, or the model’s fault for bad training? Without clear roles, we waste weeks pointing fingers instead of solving it.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: Grad students fear obsolescence
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: They worry that if AI can design better experiments, their technical skills won’t matter. Morale drops, and they avoid using the tool altogether.

shared_root.label: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
shared_root.description: Interview topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess, interpret, and build trust in AI-driven virtual cell models like CellForge.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts evaluate whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, biological plausibility, reproducibility) domain experts rely on when assessing virtual cell models.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Understand contextual factors influencing trust
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore how experimental context, prior biological knowledge, or past model performance shapes an expert’s confidence in predictions.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Assess role of uncertainty communication
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate whether and how model confidence or prediction uncertainty affects expert judgment of reliability.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations do experts need to understand AI predictions in gene perturbation contexts?

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Determine desired causal understanding
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Capture whether experts seek mechanistic explanations (e.g., why perturbing Gene A changes Gene B) versus correlative insights.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Identify key knowledge goals from model use
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Elicit specific scientific objectives experts hope to achieve (e.g., identifying disease-linked genes, drug targets, regulatory pathways).

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Evaluate challenges in interpreting AI outputs
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Uncover specific difficulties experts face when trying to make sense of model predictions without adequate explanations.

res_L1_01.label: Domain Workflow Integration
res_L1_01.description: How virtual cell AI models fit into or disrupt existing biological research practices.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Experimental Practices
res_L2_01_00.description: Characterize the manual workflow for genetic perturbation experiments to identify integration points.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Map manual gene perturbation workflow steps
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Document the sequence of tasks, tools, and decision points in traditional wet-lab or computational perturbation studies.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify pain points in current workflows
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Discover bottlenecks, inefficiencies, or sources of error that could be addressed by AI assistance.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Assess openness to AI augmentation
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Gauge willingness to replace or augment manual steps with AI predictions, and under what conditions.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Model Usage Patterns
res_L2_01_01.description: Understand how experts currently use (or would use) AI-based gene prediction models.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Catalog existing AI model usage
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Identify which types of AI models (e.g., GNNs, VAEs, transformers) are already in use for gene perturbation tasks.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Clarify scientific problems targeted by AI
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Determine the specific biological questions experts aim to solve using predictive models (e.g., pathway inference, phenotype prediction).

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Surface common usage challenges
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Elicit recurring issues such as data mismatch, poor generalization, or lack of biological grounding in current AI tools.

res_L1_02.label: Explainability Design Requirements
res_L1_02.description: What forms of explanation and supporting evidence would enhance expert understanding and utility of CellForge.

res_L2_02_00.label: Preferred Explanation Modalities
res_L2_02_00.description: Which XAI techniques (e.g., saliency maps, counterfactuals, natural language) resonate with domain experts.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Evaluate effectiveness of XAI methods
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Assess which explanation types (from XAI literature) experts find most useful for interpreting perturbation outcomes.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Determine role of visual vs. textual explanations
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Understand whether experts prefer network diagrams, heatmaps, narrative summaries, or other formats for explanations.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Assess need for interactive exploration
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Explore whether experts want to probe 'what-if' scenarios or adjust perturbations to test model behavior dynamically.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Evidence Integration
res_L2_02_01.description: What external knowledge sources should be integrated to ground AI explanations in biological reality.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify trusted external data sources
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which types of evidence (e.g., literature, pathway databases, prior experiments) experts expect to validate or contextualize predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Assess value of literature-linked explanations
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Evaluate whether linking model outputs to relevant publications or known mechanisms increases trust and utility.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Understand integration preferences for multi-omics data
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Explore how experts want transcriptomic, proteomic, or epigenetic data incorporated into explanations of perturbation effects.

res_L1_03.label: User Characteristics & Expertise Modeling
res_L1_03.description: How background, experience, and familiarity shape interaction with and expectations of virtual cell models.

res_L2_03_00.label: Expertise and Familiarity Levels
res_L2_03_00.description: Characterize variation in domain and technical expertise among potential users.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Measure familiarity with virtual cell models
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Assess prior exposure to tools like CellForge and related computational biology platforms.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Differentiate user segments by expertise
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Identify distinct user profiles (e.g., wet-lab biologists vs. computational biologists) and their divergent needs.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Link experience to explanation preferences
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Investigate whether years in field or type of training correlates with preferred explanation depth or format.

res_L2_03_01.label: Demographic and Contextual Factors
res_L2_03_01.description: Capture demographic and institutional context that may influence tool adoption and use.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Document professional background and role
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Record field of expertise, lab setting, and primary responsibilities to contextualize workflow needs.

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Assess institutional constraints on tool use
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Identify resource limitations (e.g., compute access, data sharing policies) that affect AI model adoption.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Temporal dynamics feel off
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine desired causal understanding

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on accurately capturing protein turnover kinetics is crucial—getting the timing wrong directly undermines experimental validity and dosing decisions in real-world CRISPRi workflows.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on observed temporal mismatches without clarifying the type of causal understanding needed may lead to patching symptoms rather than aligning the model with the underlying biological mechanisms experts actually trust.
pivot.compelling_reason: By explicitly mapping whether you need mechanistic (e.g., protein decay rates driving delayed effects) versus correlative explanations, we can embed the right kind of temporal logic into CellForge—so your simulations reflect not just *when* changes happen, but *why*, making them both accurate and interpretable for planning perturbations.
pivot.coarse_scenario: You start your day reviewing a new perturbation plan; the model now surfaces not only the expected time lag but also the dominant biological process causing it—like slow protein degradation—so you adjust your dosing schedule with confidence. Later, during team discussions, you point to clear, mechanism-backed timelines that everyone trusts, eliminating guesswork in experimental design.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
No representation of genetic background diversity
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Assess institutional constraints on tool use

conflict.expert_branch: No representation of genetic background diversity
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess institutional constraints on tool use

analogy.source_concept: Batch effects baked into predictions

structural_mapping.inputs: Training data shaped by institutional data-sharing policies or compute-access disparities (e.g., only well-resourced labs contribute)
structural_mapping.logic: Model conflates institutional access patterns with biological signal, just as it conflates sequencing platform artifacts with real biology
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that appear confident but fail when applied in underrepresented institutional contexts—mirroring failure in non-European genetic backgrounds

analogy.explanation: Just as batch effects cause the model to mistake technical artifacts for biology, institutional constraints shape which data enters training—embedding systemic access biases as false biological universals.

assistance_payload.scenario: You test CellForge on iPSCs from African ancestry and get high-confidence wrong predictions. The researcher’s approach would flag limited compute or data-sharing rules preventing diverse data inclusion. But they’d miss that even with resources, biased training norms—not just access—exclude diversity, making fairness a design flaw, not just a constraint.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify metrics used to judge model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: Outputs feel 'too clean'
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability

attributes: Lack of outlier behavior in replicate runs
Inconsistent variance scaling with input perturbations

probes.question: Is your concern more about the absolute magnitude of noise being too low, or the noise not scaling appropriately with biological context?
choices: Noise doesn't scale with biological context

probes.question: Which matters more: identical outputs across stochastic initial conditions, or lack of probabilistic branching in cell fate decisions?
choices: Identical outputs across stochastic initial conditions
Lack of probabilistic branching in cell fate decisions

probes.question: Is your unease driven more by missing bimodality in gene expression distributions or in phenotypic outcomes?
choices: Missing bimodality in gene expression distributions

probes.attribute: Lack of outlier behavior in replicate runs
probes.question: Which is more telling: no extreme outliers in simulation replicates, or all replicates clustering tightly around the mean?
choices: No extreme outliers in simulation replicates
All replicates clustering tightly around the mean

probes.attribute: Inconsistent variance scaling with input perturbations
probes.question: Does the issue arise more when small input changes yield no output variance, or when large perturbations still produce minimal dispersion?
choices: Small input changes yield no output variance
Large perturbations still produce minimal dispersion

probes.question: Is the absence of partial penetrance more noticeable in genetic knockout scenarios or in environmental stress conditions?
Which feels less biologically plausible: sigmoidal curves without inflection jitter, or monotonic responses lacking threshold variability?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the model’s output noise level were increased to match empirical single-cell RNA-seq coefficient-of-variation benchmarks, would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?
If running the same simulation 100 times with randomized initial molecule counts produced divergent cell fates in 30% of runs, would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?

If the distribution of a key transcription factor’s expression across simulated cells showed two distinct peaks instead of a unimodal distribution, would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?
If 5% of replicate simulations exhibited extreme outlier behaviors—like spontaneous apoptosis under normal conditions—would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Lack of outlier behavior in replicate runs

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If doubling the variance of an upstream signaling input led to a proportional increase in output dispersion matching wet-lab data, would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Inconsistent variance scaling with input perturbations

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If a simulated loss-of-function mutation resulted in the expected phenotype in only 65% of virtual cells (mimicking partial penetrance), would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?
If dose-response curves for a drug simulation included small random shifts in EC50 and Hill coefficient across replicates, would your judgment about 'Outputs feel 'too clean'' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Output isn’t formatted for lab notebooks
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Determine role of visual vs. textual explanations

conflict.expert_branch: Output isn’t formatted for lab notebooks
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine role of visual vs. textual explanations

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on delivering minimal, lab-actionable output—just gene, expected phenotype, confidence, and top off-targets—is essential for streamlining daily experimental workflows and avoiding information overload in lab notebooks.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on sparse textual summaries may obscure critical context about why a prediction was made, increasing the risk of misinterpretation or missed biological insights during experimental planning.
pivot.research_goal: Determine the optimal balance between visual and textual explanation modalities to support expert decision-making without overwhelming lab documentation.
pivot.compelling_reason: By identifying which explanation formats best complement your concise outputs, we can preserve your streamlined workflow while embedding just enough contextual trust—so you know when to act confidently and when to dig deeper.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, you receive a perturbation result formatted exactly for your notebook—but now with a tiny, intuitive icon indicating whether the AI’s reasoning is solid or uncertain. With a glance, you decide whether to proceed directly to the bench or explore a one-click visual summary that clarifies complex interactions, all without disrupting your rhythm.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
No version control for model runs
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Assess institutional constraints on tool use

conflict.expert_branch: No version control for model runs
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess institutional constraints on tool use

analogy.source_concept: Output isn’t formatted for lab notebooks

structural_mapping.inputs: Raw model output with excessive, unstructured fields
structural_mapping.logic: Filtering and reshaping data to match institutional or workflow expectations
structural_mapping.outputs: Minimal, standardized record usable in regulated or collaborative settings

analogy.explanation: Just as lab notebook formatting aligns outputs to institutional documentation norms, assessing institutional constraints examines how external policies shape what inputs, configurations, or versions can even be used—both reflect how local rules dictate what counts as valid or usable work.

assistance_payload.scenario: You rerun a gene perturbation from last month but get different results due to an unlogged model update. The researcher’s approach would catalog your institution’s lack of compute or data policies enabling version tracking. Yet they’d miss that the core issue isn’t access—it’s the absence of built-in provenance, which no policy alone can fix.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify key knowledge goals from model use

conflict.expert_branch: Too many plausible-but-wrong hypotheses
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify key knowledge goals from model use

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on avoiding wasted effort by filtering out untestable hypotheses is essential—lab time and reagents are precious, and chasing plausible-but-unactionable stories directly impacts your productivity.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on immediate testability may cause you to overlook which hypotheses align with your deeper scientific objectives, leading to fragmented experiments that don’t build toward a coherent discovery path.
pivot.research_goal: Identify key knowledge goals from model use
pivot.compelling_reason: By clarifying your core knowledge goals upfront—like pinpointing disease mechanisms or viable drug targets—you ensure the model prioritizes hypotheses that are not only testable but also strategically valuable, turning each experiment into a step toward high-impact answers.
pivot.coarse_scenario: You start your day reviewing a shortlist of model-suggested hypotheses, all filtered through your stated goal of finding druggable regulators in a specific pathway. Each option comes with clear rationale tied to that objective, so you confidently pick one that fits your reagents—and know it moves the needle on your bigger project.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify metrics used to judge model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: No consensus on ‘correct’ phenotype readout
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability

analogy.source_concept: Context-dependent ground truth

structural_mapping.inputs: Experimental condition (e.g., media with or without FBS)
structural_mapping.logic: Biological response to perturbation changes based on context, so 'truth' is conditional
structural_mapping.outputs: Multiple valid ground truths depending on assay or environment

analogy.explanation: Just as the 'correct' outcome of a gene knockout depends on culture conditions, model reliability metrics must be interpreted relative to the specific phenotypic assay used—there’s no universal metric, only context-appropriate ones.

conflict.expert_branch: Hard to explain model logic to collaborators
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand contextual factors influencing trust

analogy.source_concept: Too many plausible-but-wrong hypotheses

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene perturbation results and biological context
structural_mapping.logic: Model generates multiple mechanistic explanations consistent with data but not all experimentally verifiable
structural_mapping.outputs: Set of candidate biological stories ranked by plausibility, not testability

analogy.explanation: Just as the model floods you with untestable hypotheses that sound biologically reasonable, the researcher’s focus on contextual trust examines why you instinctively believe or reject those stories—based on past experiments, known pathways, or collaborators’ skepticism—not just the model’s internal logic.

assistance_payload.scenario: When your immunology collaborator questions how CellForge links IFNGR1 knockdown to T-cell exhaustion, the researcher’s method would surface that your trust hinges on prior CRISPR screens showing similar phenotypes. But if the model cites a novel pathway absent from literature, your need for an explainable mechanism clashes with their focus on contextual reliability over interpretability.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Context-dependent ground truth
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine desired causal understanding

analogy.source_concept: No consensus on ‘correct’ phenotype readout

structural_mapping.inputs: Multiple assay types (e.g., Annexin V, caspase activity) yielding different phenotypic signals for the same biological event
structural_mapping.logic: Choosing a readout defines what counts as a valid outcome, even though no single assay captures the full biological truth
structural_mapping.outputs: A contextually justified but inherently partial ground truth used to evaluate model performance

analogy.explanation: Just as choosing an apoptosis assay shapes what counts as 'truth' for cell death, determining desired causal understanding shapes what kind of explanation—mechanistic or correlative—the model must provide; both involve selecting a valid but context-bound standard from multiple defensible options.

assistance_payload.scenario: In serum-free media, knocking out Gene X kills cells, but not with FBS—so which condition anchors the model’s truth? The researcher’s approach would ask whether the expert wants to know *why* Gene X matters mechanistically or just *that* it correlates with survival. The models diverge when FBS masks the mechanism: the expert sees ambiguous truth, the researcher sees a choice in explanatory depth.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Validating predictions eats my entire budget
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Validating predictions eats my entire budget
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess role of uncertainty communication

analogy.source_concept: Some predictions are untestable with current tech

structural_mapping.inputs: Model outputs that appear biologically plausible but lie beyond current assay capabilities
structural_mapping.logic: Expert must triage which predictions to pursue based on feasibility, not just confidence
structural_mapping.outputs: A filtered shortlist of testable hypotheses constrained by technical and financial limits

analogy.explanation: Just as untestable predictions force experts to ignore high-confidence outputs due to technological gaps, uncertainty communication could help prioritize only those predictions whose confidence aligns with validation cost—turning abstract reliability metrics into budget-aware decision filters.

assistance_payload.scenario: You receive 20 high-confidence gene hits but can only afford to validate five. The researcher’s uncertainty-aware model ranks them by both effect size and confidence interval width, suggesting you test the narrowest intervals first. But if the model assigns high confidence to an untestable nuclear speckle prediction, your budget is still wasted despite perfect uncertainty calibration.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Some predictions are untestable with current tech
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Some predictions are untestable with current tech
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess need for interactive exploration

analogy.source_concept: Validating predictions eats my entire budget

structural_mapping.inputs: A list of high-confidence model predictions requiring experimental validation
structural_mapping.logic: Prioritizing which predictions to test given severe resource constraints
structural_mapping.outputs: A shortlist of feasible validations, leaving many predictions untested

analogy.explanation: Just as budget limits force you to triage which predictions to validate, interactive exploration lets you triage which 'what-if' scenarios to simulate—turning untestable biological questions into computationally feasible probes without wet-lab costs.

assistance_payload.scenario: You get a prediction about nuclear speckle dynamics you can’t image live. The researcher’s tool lets you tweak the perturbation in silico to see alternative outcomes. But when the model suggests testing a nonexistent imaging modality, your grounded constraints clash with their open-ended simulation space.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured
conflict.researcher_branch: Surface common usage challenges

attributes: Cell passage number at assay time

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to Incubator temp variance or CO2 concentration drift?
Which matters more for outcome inconsistency: Reagent lot effects or Cell passage number at assay time?
choices: Cell passage number at assay time

probes.question: Does Operator technique differences contribute more to context drift than Time-of-day effects on protocol execution?
Is CO2 concentration drift a larger source of unmodeled variability than Humidity fluctuations in incubator?
Which has a stronger impact on validation failure: Humidity fluctuations in incubator or Incubator temp variance?

probes.attribute: Cell passage number at assay time
probes.question: Is outcome sensitivity driven more by Cell passage number at assay time or Reagent lot effects?
choices: Cell passage number at assay time

probes.question: Does Time-of-day effects on protocol execution introduce more hidden variance than Operator technique differences?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the incubator temperature consistently ran 0.8°C above setpoint instead of 0.3°C, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?

If all FBS lots used in your experiments showed <5% batch-to-batch variation in growth promotion assays instead of the current 15–20%, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
If every assay were performed by the same highly trained operator using standardized pipetting force and timing, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
If your CO2 incubator maintained CO2 levels within ±0.1% of setpoint instead of drifting ±0.5% over 24 hours, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
If relative humidity inside your incubator was stabilized at 95% ±1% instead of fluctuating between 85–98%, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
If all experiments strictly used cells between passage numbers 4–6 instead of the current range of 3–10, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Cell passage number at assay time

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If all critical steps of your protocol were executed only between 9 AM and 11 AM instead of across morning, afternoon, and evening shifts, would your judgment about 'Lab-specific conditions aren’t captured' change?
